skill.name: video-edit
skill.description: Edit existing video on RunComfy — this skill is a smart router that matches the user's intent to the right edit model in the RunComfy catalog. Picks Wan 2.7 Edit-Video (general restyle / background swap / packaging swap, identity + motion preservation), Kling 2.6 Pro Motion Control (transfer precise motion from a reference video to a target character), or Lucy Edit Restyle (lightweight identity-stable restyle / outfit swap). Bundles each model's documented prompting patterns so the skill gets sharper edits without burning iterations on the wrong model. Calls `runcomfy run <vendor>/<model>/<endpoint>` through the local RunComfy CLI.
skill.summary: Smart router for AI video editing that matches user intent to the best model on RunComfy
skill.author: runcomfy-com

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged 101 potential security issues. After semantic evaluation, all findings are false positives. The skill is a legitimate video editing router that invokes the RunComfy CLI to access AI video editing models. External commands are documentation examples in markdown code blocks. Network URLs are to RunComfy services. Path traversals are documentation placeholders. Token storage is documented with proper file permissions (0600). No evidence of malicious intent or actual security risks.

medium_findings.title: CLI Invocations in Documentation
medium_findings.description: The skill contains 66 instances of backtick command examples showing `runcomfy run` invocations. These are documentation examples in markdown code blocks, not actual code execution. The skill is designed to guide an AI agent to invoke the CLI with appropriate parameters based on user intent.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: All backtick patterns appear in markdown code blocks (```bash) as documentation examples showing CLI usage patterns. The skill describes how to route user requests to appropriate models, not execute arbitrary commands.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs to RunComfy Services
low_findings.description: The skill contains URLs to runcomfy.com and runcomfy.net domains. These are legitimate service endpoints documented as part of the skill's functionality for video editing routing.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All URLs point to documented RunComfy services (runcomfy.com, runcomfy.net, docs.runcomfy.com). These are legitimate service links for the video editing platform.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal Patterns in Documentation
low_findings.description: The skill uses `<absolute/path>` as a placeholder in CLI examples. This is documentation showing how to specify output directories, not actual file access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The placeholder `<absolute/path>` is standard documentation syntax indicating where users should provide their own path. The skill does not construct paths from user input or perform arbitrary file access.

low_findings.title: Token Storage in Hidden Directory
low_findings.description: The skill documents that `runcomfy login` writes the API token to `~/.config/runcomfy/token.json` with mode 0600 permissions. This is documented as a security measure, not a vulnerability.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill explicitly documents that the token file is created with owner-only permissions (0600), which is a security best practice. The skill also documents the RUNCOMFY_TOKEN env var alternative for CI environments.

content.user_title: Edit videos with AI - route to the right model
content.value_statement: Video editing requires matching the right AI model to your goal. This skill intelligently routes requests to Wan 2.7, Kling, or Lucy based on whether you need restyle, motion control, or identity-stable edits.
actual_capabilities: Route video editing requests to the optimal AI model based on user intent
Apply background swaps and packaging changes with Wan 2.7 Edit-Video
Transfer precise motion from reference videos to target characters with Kling 2.6
Perform lightweight outfit swaps and identity-stable restyles with Lucy Edit
Preserve face, pose, and lip movement across editing operations
Generate video edits using natural language prompts through RunComfy CLI
limitations: Wan 2.7 Edit limited to 2-10 second clips at 1080p maximum
Kling Motion Control caps at 10s for image orientation, 30s for video orientation
Lucy Edit Restyle capped at 720p with no aspect ratio control
Cannot blend multiple models in a single edit operation

use_cases.title: Marketing teams swapping product backgrounds
use_cases.description: A marketing professional needs to change the background of a product video while preserving the camera motion and product appearance. The skill routes to Wan 2.7 for background swap with motion preservation.

use_cases.title: Content creators transferring motion between videos
use_cases.description: A content creator wants to apply the dance moves from a reference video to their animated character. The skill routes to Kling 2.6 Motion Control for precise motion mapping.

use_cases.title: Video producers performing identity-stable outfit swaps
use_cases.description: A video producer needs to change an actor's outfit while keeping their face and body movements consistent. The skill routes to Lucy Edit Restyle for lightweight localized changes.

prompt_templates.title: Basic video restyle
prompt_templates.prompt: Edit this video to show the subject in a modern office setting with neutral lighting. Preserve the face, pose, and lip movement.
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants to change the background of a talking-head video

prompt_templates.title: Packaging design swap
prompt_templates.prompt: Replace the packaging design on the product in the video using this reference image. Keep the original framing and hand movement.
prompt_templates.scenario: User provides a reference image for design transfer

prompt_templates.title: Motion transfer from reference
prompt_templates.prompt: Apply the dancing motion from the reference video to the character in the target image. Keep the original sound.
prompt_templates.scenario: User has a reference video for motion and a target image or video

prompt_templates.title: Outfit swap with identity preservation
prompt_templates.prompt: Change the outfit to professional business attire. Preserve the face and motion throughout the video.
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants a localized outfit change while maintaining character identity

output_examples.input: Edit this video to show a warm golden hour lighting with the subject in a desert landscape
output_examples.output: The skill routes to Lucy Edit Restyle and produces a video with warm, golden lighting applied consistently while preserving the subject's face and body movements.

output_examples.input: Transfer the salsa dancing motion from the reference clip to the target character
output_examples.output: The skill routes to Kling 2.6 Motion Control and generates a video of the target character performing the salsa dance with temporal coherence maintained.

output_examples.input: Swap the background to a beach sunset while keeping the speaker's face and gestures
output_examples.output: The skill routes to Wan 2.7 Edit-Video and produces a video with the beach sunset background while preserving identity and motion.

best_practices: Lead with preservation goals in prompts: specify what to keep (face, pose, motion) before describing the change
Use one edit direction per call: compound edits across multiple dimensions reduce motion quality
Match the model to the intent: use Wan for restyle/background, Kling for motion transfer, Lucy for lightweight identity edits
anti_patterns: Avoid compound edits in a single prompt: asking for background change plus outfit change plus lighting in one call causes motion drift
Do not use reference images for general restyle: reference_image should only be used for direct design transfer like packaging swaps
Avoid full character replacement: Lucy and other models are built for localized style modifications, not swapping subjects entirely

faq.question: Which video editing model should I use?
faq.answer: Use Wan 2.7 for general restyle and background swaps, Kling 2.6 for transferring motion from a reference video, and Lucy for lightweight identity-stable edits like outfit changes.

faq.question: What video formats are supported?
faq.answer: Formats vary by model: Wan and Lucy support MP4/MOV/WEBM/GIF. Check the RunComfy documentation for the latest format support and size limits.

faq.question: How long can the input video be?
faq.answer: Wan 2.7 supports 2-10 second clips. Kling supports 10s (image orientation) or 30s (video orientation). Lucy has variable limits based on the model tier.

faq.question: Can I preserve the original audio?
faq.answer: Yes. For Wan 2.7, set audio_setting to origin to preserve source audio. For Kling, set keep_original_sound to true.

faq.question: What resolution options are available?
faq.answer: Wan 2.7 supports 720p and 1080p. Lucy Edit Restyle is capped at 720p. Kling resolution depends on the orientation and model tier.

faq.question: How do I troubleshoot failed video edits?
faq.answer: Check exit codes: 64 means bad CLI args, 65 means bad input JSON, 69 means upstream server error, 75 means retryable timeout, 77 means authentication issue. See docs.runcomfy.com for detailed troubleshooting.
